---
title: "Intent-to-Execution Contracts"
chapter_id: "intent-to-execution-contracts"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "viea"
  - "talos"
  - "software_magic_grimoire"
  - "genesiscode"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Intent-to-Execution Contracts

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `intent-to-execution-contracts` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `viea`, `talos`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `viea`; supporting: `talos`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `viea`, `talos`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`, `moecot`; raw cache: `viea`, `talos`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

Human goals need a typed path into execution that preserves requirements, artifacts, verification, deployment, and feedback.

Part I produced governed intent: purpose, authority ceilings, protected invariants, rights, and self-improvement boundaries. Part II begins when that intent must become work. VIEA's central move is that intent should not jump straight to a model response or a tool call. It should become a chain of typed artifacts: intent contract, command contract, plan, typed job, runtime action, artifact, verification record, feedback, and residuals.

This chapter is the bridge between governance and cognition. Planning, memory, routing, execution, and evidence can only do disciplined work if the request they receive has a durable identity, constraints, acceptance conditions, side-effect limits, and an artifact trace.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A model response is not an execution contract; it lacks lifecycle, acceptance, artifact identity, and side-effect control.

The common failure is to treat fluent text as completed work. A response can sound responsive while hiding missing approvals, untracked source boundaries, unverifiable artifacts, unexecuted tests, or side effects that were never authorized. Talos sharpens the point: work should enter a job lifecycle, not a loose conversational state. GenesisCode adds the software version of the same boundary: proposals, effects, replay logs, and obligations must be separated.

The stack needs a contract boundary because later layers should not infer authority from prose. A planner can decompose a contract. A runtime can enforce a contract. A verifier can check a contract. A bare response cannot reliably do any of those things.

## Core Claim

[intent-to-execution-contracts.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] The stack should transform intent into execution contracts before any tool or runtime action occurs.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture, Talos Protocol, Software Magic Grimoire, GenesisCode, and MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is an Intent-to-Execution Trace.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Human intent"] --> B["Intent contract"]
  B --> C["Command contract"]
  C --> D["Plan graph"]
  D --> E["Typed jobs"]
  E --> F{"Authority + approval gate"}
  F -- "allow" --> G["Runtime adapter / tool"]
  F -- "deny or missing" --> H["Residual / escalation"]
  G --> I["Artifact"]
  I --> J["Verification record"]
  J --> K["Feedback + evidence ledger"]
  H --> K
```

- Represent command contracts with inputs, constraints, artifacts, tools, evidence, approvals, and feedback.
- Route contracts through planning, memory, verification, and execution layers.
- Attach result artifacts back to the claim and evidence ledger.

The trace is append-only at the level that matters: each transition either produces the next artifact or records why it could not. If a contract cannot be planned, that is a residual. If a job cannot run because approval is missing, that is a residual. If an artifact is produced but verification fails, that is a residual with a repair path.

This is why the book treats feedback as part of execution, not as a postscript. Result artifacts should update claims, regression suites, tools, and future plans. Failures should become residuals, not disappear into the next prompt.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Intent-to-Execution Trace.

- Intent contracts feed planning.
- Execution consumes typed jobs derived from contracts.
- Evidence records contract satisfaction.

Minimum fields:

- `trace_id`
- `intent_contract`
- `command_contract`
- `plan_graph`
- `typed_jobs`
- `authority_transitions`
- `artifacts`
- `verification_records`
- `delivery_or_deployment`
- `feedback_refs`
- `residuals`

Intent contracts feed planning. Command contracts bind procedure and outputs. Plan graphs decompose the work. Typed jobs carry executable units. Authority transitions control side effects. Verification records decide whether the artifact satisfies the original intent.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Contract constraints survive compilation.
- Side effects require explicit execution authority.
- Artifacts remain linked to source intent.

The key invariant is preservation across compilation: the plan, job, artifact, and verification record must still be traceable to the parent intent. If the system cannot show that lineage, it has not executed the intent; it has only produced something near it.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Response mistaken for completed work.
- Artifact identity lost.
- Approval bypass.

The first failure is especially dangerous for a living book: a chapter can be drafted without the evidence matrix, source trace, or validation state being updated. Artifact identity loss breaks audit and repair. Approval bypass turns planning into execution authority. Each failure should block promotion until the trace is repaired.

## Minimal implementation

A command-contract schema and one end-to-end intent-to-artifact trace.

The smallest useful implementation is a `command_contract` schema plus an `intent_execution_trace` fixture that links an intent contract, plan graph placeholder, typed job, authority transition, artifact, verification record, and residuals. Passing validation only proves trace shape; it does not prove the work satisfied the intent.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Contract field completeness test | Check that required objective, constraints, outputs, verification, approvals, and failure behavior are present. | planned; not run |
| Constraint preservation test | Check that constraints in the intent contract remain visible in command, plan, job, and artifact records. | planned; not run |
| Artifact traceability test | Check that produced artifacts point back to intent, command, verification, and residual records. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:intent_execution.contracts.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.IntentToExecution` | A compiled execution job preserves the parent contract constraints. | planned |
| `lean:intent_execution.contracts.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.IntentToExecution` | An execution job without required approval cannot transition to running. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Software Magic Grimoire | command_contracts_promptcraft | Structured command contracts and semantic density of software words. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | executable_specification | Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Intent-to-Execution Contracts is the stack's first operational handoff. It turns human intent into a traceable chain of contracts, plans, jobs, artifacts, verification, feedback, and residuals.

The point is not bureaucracy. It is continuity: requirements, authority, artifacts, and evidence have to survive the trip from purpose to action.
